theories.name: Mental Health AI → Broader Access → Reduced Suffering
theories.impactEstimate: ~1B people globally with mental health conditions (WHO). ~75% in LMICs receive no treatment. In the US, ~160M people live in mental health professional shortage areas.
theories.investmentCase: Investment level unknown despite 1B affected people — AI therapy tools exist but no long-term study shows clinically meaningful improvement, so funders can't assess ROI.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal RCT (12+ months) comparing AI mental health tools vs. waitlist control in mental health professional shortage areas, measuring sustained symptom improvement and whether users are people who genuinely lack access to human therapists.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI chatbots and digital tools can broaden mental health access for the ~1B people with mental health conditions globally, 75% of whom receive no treatment. Evidence is moderate at the endpoints — AI can deliver therapeutic interventions (Woebot, Wysa) and treatment works when received. The chain breaks at Steps 3-4: no long-term evidence that AI tools produce clinically meaningful improvement, and no measurement of whether they actually reduce the treatment gap at population level.
theories.keyConfounders: High user dropout rates (most drop off within 2 weeks), safety concerns (Character.AI suicide case), unclear whether tools reach underserved vs. already-served populations, no long-term follow-up data, regulatory gaps for non-FDA-cleared tools. CHAIN-NEGATING: Retention/dropout — most users abandon mental health apps within 2 weeks. If tools can't retain users long enough to deliver therapeutic benefit, the entire chain collapses.

claims.name: AI can deliver therapeutic interventions or screening at scale via chatbots, apps, and digital tools
claims.keyEvidence: Woebot (CBT chatbot) showed symptom reduction in an RCT for depression and anxiety (Stanford, 2017). Wysa has 5M+ users and NHS endorsement. Crisis Text Line uses AI to prioritize high-risk conversations. But most tools are not FDA-cleared and evidence base is small — typically single-site studies with short follow-up.
claims.notes: Promising early results but the evidence base is thin compared to other healthcare AI domains. More and larger RCTs needed, especially with active control groups (AI therapy vs. human therapy, not just vs. waitlist).

claims.name: People who wouldn't otherwise access mental health care actually use these AI tools
claims.keyEvidence: High initial engagement: Wysa reports 5M+ users, many in countries with severe therapist shortages. Crisis Text Line handles 200K+ conversations/month. But retention is a major problem — most mental health app users drop off within 2 weeks. Unclear whether AI tools reach people who truly lack access vs. people who already have access and find apps convenient.
claims.notes: Engagement ≠ access expansion. Need studies specifically measuring whether AI mental health tools reach underserved populations (rural, low-income, stigma-deterred) who wouldn't otherwise get care.

claims.name: AI mental health interventions produce clinically meaningful symptom improvement
claims.keyEvidence: Woebot's RCT showed statistically significant but modest symptom reduction. No long-term follow-up studies (>6 months). No head-to-head trials vs. human therapists. Character.AI chatbot implicated in a teenager's suicide, raising safety concerns. The gap between 'users report feeling better' and 'clinically meaningful, sustained improvement' is wide.
claims.notes: The safety question is as important as the efficacy question. AI therapy tools could help millions or cause harm at scale. Rigorous, long-term RCTs with safety monitoring are urgently needed and highly fundable.

claims.name: AI-assisted mental health care reduces the treatment gap (the difference between people who need care and people who receive it)
claims.keyEvidence: WHO estimates 75% of people with mental health conditions in low-income countries receive no treatment. Global therapist shortage is severe. But no studies have measured whether AI tools actually reduce the treatment gap at a population level. It's plausible but completely unmeasured.
claims.notes: The treatment gap is one of the most important problems in global health. AI could help, but we have zero evidence it does. Investment in measurement plus time for deployment to scale are both needed.

claims.name: Reduced treatment gap leads to reduced population-level mental health burden
claims.keyEvidence: Well-established that treatment works: CBT and SSRIs have strong evidence for depression and anxiety. The WHO Mental Health Gap Action Programme showed that scaling basic mental health care in low-resource settings improves outcomes. If people get effective treatment, outcomes improve — the question is whether Steps 1-4 deliver effective treatment.
claims.notes: This link is well-supported by mental health research. The chain breaks at the earlier steps (does AI therapy work? does it reach the right people?), not at this final outcome link.

theories.name: AI Operations → Nonprofit/NGO Effectiveness → Amplified Mission Impact
theories.impactEstimate: ~10M nonprofits globally. If AI improves operational effectiveness by 10% (fundraising, grant writing, reporting, program delivery), aggregate impact across the social sector could redirect billions in currently wasted capacity toward mission delivery.
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because no single nonprofit's 10% improvement makes headlines. The aggregation claim (many small improvements = transformational) is plausible but untested — and the IT revolution precedent is discouraging: previous technology waves improved nonprofit operations without producing measurable aggregate mission impact.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a controlled study across 50-100 nonprofits measuring: (1) whether AI operational tools produce measurable improvements in mission outcomes (not just operational efficiency), (2) whether saved time goes to mission delivery vs. more admin/meetings/scope creep, and (3) whether the IT revolution precedent holds — does AI break the pattern of operational improvement without mission impact?
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims that AI tools for operations (fundraising, grant writing, reporting, donor management) can make nonprofits and NGOs meaningfully more effective, compounding across ~10M organizations globally into transformational aggregate impact. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI demonstrably improves individual operational tasks (grant writing speed, donor segmentation). The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no study measures whether AI-driven operational improvements translate to better mission outcomes, and the aggregation claim (many small improvements = transformational) is entirely unmeasured.
theories.keyConfounders: Nonprofit technology adoption barriers, digital divide across org sizes, operational improvement ≠ mission improvement, AI tool costs relative to nonprofit budgets, measurement difficulty for mission outcomes. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Operational efficiency ≠ mission effectiveness is a well-documented problem in nonprofit management (Kaplan 2001). Organizations often redirect saved time to more admin, meetings, or scope creep — not to mission delivery. The chain assumes a direct link that nonprofit management literature has shown doesn't hold without deliberate strategy. (2) The IT revolution already tested this hypothesis — computers, email, CRM systems, and cloud tools all improved nonprofit operations without producing measurable aggregate mission impact. AI may follow the same pattern: real operational improvement, no measurable mission change. (3) AI tools may homogenize nonprofit strategies — if every organization uses AI for grant writing and donor management, proposals and strategies converge, reducing the diversity of approaches that makes the social sector resilient. Funders may see identical AI-written proposals rather than authentic organizational voices.

claims.name: AI tools improve nonprofit operational tasks (grant writing, donor management, reporting, program delivery)
claims.keyEvidence: AI demonstrably improves individual operational tasks: ChatGPT and similar tools reduce grant writing time by 30-50% (anecdotal reports from nonprofit leaders), AI donor segmentation improves fundraising targeting, and AI reporting tools automate compliance documentation. However, evidence is concentrated in larger, tech-savvy organizations.
claims.notes: Step 1. The capability is real but adoption is uneven. Most evidence is anecdotal or from vendor claims rather than independent studies.

claims.name: AI-driven operational improvements translate to better mission outcomes for nonprofits
claims.keyEvidence: No study has measured whether AI-driven operational improvements (faster grant writing, better donor targeting) translate to better mission outcomes (more people served, better program quality). The assumption that operational efficiency → mission effectiveness is untested and may not hold — organizations could redirect saved time to more admin rather than more mission.
claims.notes: Step 2. This is the critical evidence gap. Operational improvement ≠ mission improvement is a known problem in nonprofit management literature (Kaplan 2001).

claims.name: Aggregate 10% improvements across ~10M organizations compound to transformational societal impact
claims.keyEvidence: The aggregation claim is entirely unmeasured. No framework exists for measuring compound impact across the nonprofit sector. The logic is plausible — small improvements across a large base can be transformational — but historical precedent is mixed. The IT revolution improved nonprofit operations without producing measurable aggregate mission impact.
claims.notes: Step 3. This is the unique cross-domain insight: many small improvements may add up to transformational impact. But no one has measured or funded measurement of this aggregation effect.

theories.name: AI Assessment → Faster Feedback → Improved Learning
theories.impactEstimate: ~69M teacher shortage globally (UNESCO). Teachers spend ~30–40% of time on assessment and grading. AI assessment could free ~10–15 hours/week per teacher for instruction. ~1.6B students could receive more frequent, personalized feedback.
theories.investmentCase: Lightly funded but evidence-blind — no RCT has compared AI-generated vs. expert teacher feedback on actual learning outcomes, so investment is chasing speed without proof it improves learning.
theories.investableInsight: Fund RCTs comparing AI-generated feedback vs. expert teacher feedback on learning outcomes across subjects, measuring both learning gains and total teacher workload impact.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI assessment can free teachers from grading (~30–40% of their time) and provide faster, more frequent feedback to ~1.6B students. Evidence is strong at Step 2 — speed advantage is inherent to the technology — and moderate across other steps. The chain weakens at Steps 3–4: education research shows feedback quality matters more than speed, and AI assessment may create new work (auditing AI grades, handling edge cases) rather than reducing total teacher workload.
theories.keyConfounders: Feedback quality vs. speed tradeoff, AI inability to assess creative and open-ended work, teacher workload shift rather than reduction, student trust in AI grading, assessment gaming. CHAIN-NEGATING: Pedagogical shift toward competencies AI can't assess (creativity, critical thinking, collaboration) — AI may be optimizing for an assessment paradigm being abandoned.

claims.name: AI can assess student work with useful accuracy across formats
claims.keyEvidence: Automated essay scoring (AES) systems show 0.7-0.9 correlation with human raters (Shermis & Burstein 2013). ETS's e-rater used in GRE scoring. LLM-based assessment (GPT-4, Claude) shows strong performance on structured problems (math, code, short answer) but weaker on creative, open-ended, and subjective assessments. Consistent finding: AI assessment works well for convergent tasks, poorly for divergent ones.
claims.investmentNotes: For-profit: Turnitin (acquired for $1.75B by Advance Publications in 2019, $203M revenue in 2024, 71M students globally). Gradescope (acquired by Turnitin, $5.35M VC raised from Reach Capital, K9 Ventures, Bloomberg Beta). ETS e-rater used in GRE scoring. CodeGrade for programming assessment. Philanthropic: Limited — most assessment AI research is commercially funded. Academic research on AES (Shermis & Burstein 2013) but not heavily philanthropically funded. Government: Some state education departments investing in AI-assisted assessment but mostly procurement, not R&D.
claims.notes: The technology works for certain assessment types today. The open question is whether AI can reliably assess higher-order thinking, creativity, and collaborative skills — the competencies education systems increasingly prioritize.

claims.name: AI assessment provides substantially faster feedback than traditional methods
claims.keyEvidence: Self-evident from the technology: AI provides near-instant feedback vs. days or weeks for human grading. Studies of learning management systems with automated feedback show response times of seconds vs. average teacher turnaround of 1-2 weeks (Hattie & Timperley 2007). Platforms like Gradescope, Turnitin, and CodeGrade already demonstrate this at scale in higher education.
claims.investmentNotes: Self-evident technology advantage — speed is inherent to AI. Platforms demonstrating at scale: Gradescope (50-70% grading time reduction), Turnitin (71M students globally), CodeGrade, Georgia Tech's Jill Watson AI TA. The investment here is in the platforms themselves (Turnitin $1.75B acquisition, Gradescope, LMS integrations), not in measuring whether speed improves outcomes. Not a gap — the technology delivers faster feedback by definition.
claims.notes: This is the strongest link in the chain — speed advantage is inherent to the technology. The real question is whether faster feedback is better feedback (next step).

claims.name: Faster, more frequent feedback loops improve student learning and retention
claims.keyEvidence: Education research consistently shows feedback is one of the highest-impact interventions (Hattie 2009, effect size ~0.7). But feedback quality matters more than speed — poorly formulated feedback can be counterproductive. Limited evidence on whether AI-generated feedback is as pedagogically effective as expert teacher feedback. Some studies suggest AI feedback is helpful for procedural learning but less effective for conceptual understanding (Narciss et al. 2014).
claims.investmentNotes: Limited independent research on whether AI-generated feedback is as pedagogically effective as expert teacher feedback. Hattie (2009) established feedback as high-impact intervention (effect size ~0.7) but this was pre-AI. Narciss et al. (2014) showed AI feedback helps procedural learning but is less effective for conceptual understanding. Most investment is in building faster feedback tools (Heavy at Steps 1-2), not in measuring whether faster feedback produces better learning. High-value gap: RCTs comparing AI feedback vs. expert teacher feedback on actual learning outcomes.
claims.notes: Gap between 'faster' and 'better.' Education research is clear that feedback timing matters, but also that feedback specificity, tone, and pedagogical framing matter more. AI can be fast, but can it be pedagogically wise? RCTs comparing AI feedback vs. expert teacher feedback are a high-value investment.

claims.name: AI assessment scales without proportional increase in teacher workload
claims.keyEvidence: Platforms like Gradescope report 50-70% reduction in grading time for participating instructors. Georgia Tech's AI teaching assistant (Jill Watson) handled routine student questions without increasing instructor workload. But some studies show AI assessment creates new work: reviewing AI outputs, handling edge cases, managing student disputes about AI grades (Popenici & Kerr 2017). Net workload impact is unclear.
claims.investmentNotes: Gradescope reports 50-70% grading time reduction. Georgia Tech's Jill Watson handled routine questions. But some studies show AI assessment creates new work: reviewing AI outputs, handling edge cases, managing student disputes (Popenici & Kerr 2017). Net workload impact is unclear — limited investment in measuring total teacher workload change rather than just grading time. Potential backfire: teachers may spend less time grading but more time auditing AI grades.
claims.notes: Potential for a backfire effect where AI creates different work rather than less work. Teachers may spend less time grading but more time auditing AI grades, updating rubrics for AI, and handling student concerns about AI fairness. Need studies measuring total teacher workload impact, not just grading time.

theories.name: AI Optimization → Metric Gaming → Mission Drift
theories.impactEstimate: Goodhart's Law applied to AI optimization: when organizations use AI to optimize measurable outcomes, they systematically shift resources toward what's measurable rather than what matters. Affects every sector where AI-driven metrics guide decisions — healthcare quality scores, educational test scores, climate offset credits, employment placement rates.
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because AI optimization is embedded in the commercial AI market — every AI dashboard and analytics tool drives metric gaming as a side effect. The causing activity ($211B+ AI market) is heavily funded; measuring whether metric improvements reflect real impact is unfunded. Organizations are deploying AI optimization without measuring whether it's improving outcomes or just improving metrics — automating Goodhart's Law at scale.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a cross-sector study comparing AI-optimized vs. traditionally-managed programs on BOTH metric improvement AND independent outcome assessment, quantifying the gap between the two. Use existing data (IRS 990s, program reports, government performance data). Additionally: build independent outcome assessment infrastructure that operates outside AI-optimized metric systems — measuring what matters, not just what's measured. This is one of the more actionable investable insights in the gap map.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain identifies how AI optimization of measurable outcomes causes organizations to drift toward what's measurable rather than what matters — Goodhart's Law at institutional scale. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — metric gaming is well-documented across sectors (teaching to the test, hospital readmission gaming, police crime stat manipulation). The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: whether AI accelerates metric gaming beyond historical levels is unmeasured, and the net effect (AI-driven improvement minus AI-driven gaming) is unknown.
theories.keyConfounders: Pre-existing metric gaming independent of AI, difficulty defining 'what matters' vs. what's measured, organizational incentives aligned with metrics not outcomes, auditor capacity to detect gaming. Chain-negating (potential): AI might IMPROVE outcome measurement, not just proxy gaming — if AI can measure previously-unmeasurable outcomes (sentiment analysis, satellite imagery, etc.), it could shift organizations toward better metrics. Counter: creating better measures is a deliberate expensive activity; gaming existing proxies is the default that happens automatically. Organizational awareness of Goodhart's Law is increasing, which may partially self-correct.

claims.name: AI optimization systems optimize for measurable proxies rather than actual mission outcomes
claims.keyEvidence: Goodhart's Law ('when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure') is one of the most robust findings in social science. AI amplifies this by automating optimization toward proxy metrics at scale and speed. Well-documented in tech (engagement metrics driving content quality down), education (teaching to the test), and healthcare (coding optimization). The mechanism is well-understood.
claims.notes: Step 1. Not a gap — this is a known, well-documented phenomenon. AI simply accelerates it.

claims.name: Metric optimization systematically shifts organizational behavior away from mission-aligned activities
claims.keyEvidence: Mission drift from metric pressure is documented in nonprofits (Ebrahim 2003, Weisbrod 2004) and government agencies (Hood 2006 on targets in UK public services). AI-specific mission drift is not yet studied, but the mechanism is clear: if AI dashboards and optimization tools emphasize fundraising efficiency, grant compliance metrics, or output counts, organizations will allocate resources accordingly — potentially at the expense of harder-to-measure impact.
claims.notes: Step 2. The investable gap: a comparative study of AI-optimized vs. non-AI-optimized nonprofits, measuring mission alignment over time. Could be done with existing data (IRS 990s, program reports) plus surveys.

claims.name: Sector-wide metric gaming reduces the overall social impact of philanthropic and government spending
claims.keyEvidence: No direct evidence of AI-driven sector-wide mission drift. The UK public services experience under New Labour (2000s target culture) is the closest parallel — well-documented gaming of NHS waiting time targets, police crime statistics, and school league tables that undermined service quality. If AI accelerates this dynamic across the social sector, the aggregate impact on social outcomes could be substantial but is unmeasured.
claims.notes: Step 3. The systemic concern. Individual organizations gaming metrics is a local problem; if AI makes it universal, it's a sector-wide failure mode.

theories.name: Data Center Growth → Grid Strain → Delayed Decarbonization
theories.impactEstimate: IEA projects global data center electricity consumption could double by 2030. US data center electricity could triple by 2028. If this growth is met primarily by fossil fuels, it could add 24-44 Mt CO2/year by 2030 and delay grid decarbonization timelines by years.
theories.investmentCase: Unmeasured because no one is tracking the net balance — $200B+/year in hyperscaler capex drives demand while ~$10M in academic modeling tries to measure the consequences. Funders can't price the decarbonization risk because the most basic accounting hasn't been done.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a comprehensive net-impact accounting framework that measures the total emissions balance of AI infrastructure: new fossil capacity built for data centers, renewable PPA additionality (physical delivery vs. RECs), grid displacement effects, and carbon lock-in risk assessment. The deliverable is a single dashboard answering: 'Is AI infrastructure growth net-positive or net-negative for grid decarbonization?' — giving policymakers and investors an evidence base that currently does not exist.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain identifies how exponentially growing AI compute demand drives new fossil fuel infrastructure that locks in emissions for decades. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — data center electricity demand is growing ~30% annually, already 4.4% of US consumption. The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: while new gas plants are being built for data centers, tech companies are also signing large renewable PPAs, and the net balance between clean and dirty capacity varies by region and remains unmeasured.
theories.keyConfounders: Tech company renewable PPA additionality, SMR and long-duration storage deployment timeline, regional grid mix variation, corporate vs. actual energy sourcing (RECs vs. physical delivery), policy environment for fossil fuel retirement. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) AI IS the harm — this chain is unique because the technology being assessed is the direct cause of the problem, not a potential solution. Every step is driven by AI compute demand, and at no step does AI offer a fix. (2) Demand elasticity from cost reductions — as inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022, usage scales faster than infrastructure projections. Current IEA demand projections may be conservative. (3) The net emissions balance is unknown — $200B+/year creates the demand while ~$10M in academic modeling tries to measure the consequences. No entity is tracking whether total AI infrastructure is net-positive or net-negative for decarbonization.

claims.name: AI training and inference demand is growing exponentially, requiring massive new data center capacity
claims.keyEvidence: IEA (2025) projects accelerated server electricity consumption growing 30% annually. Goldman Sachs projects 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. Major cloud providers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) each planning 10+ GW of new data center capacity. US data center electricity already 4.4% of national consumption (LBNL 2023). Multiple new natural gas plants being built specifically to power data centers.
claims.investmentNotes: Heavy investment creating this harm. Google ($75B), Microsoft ($80B), Amazon ($75B+), Meta ($60-65B) each investing massively in AI infrastructure in 2025. Goldman Sachs projects 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. Each major cloud provider planning 10+ GW of new capacity. US data center electricity already 4.4% of national consumption (LBNL 2023). Washington Post (Feb 2026) documents tech companies building private 'shadow power grid' with on-site gas and solar plants. IEA projects global data center electricity could double by 2030.
claims.notes: Step 1. Firmly established and accelerating. The demand growth is empirically documented, not projected. The question is whether this demand is met with clean or dirty energy (Steps 2-3).

claims.name: Data center energy demand growth outpaces renewable energy deployment, requiring fossil fuel generation
claims.keyEvidence: MIT Technology Review (2025) documents data centers trending toward gas to meet immediate needs. Several US utilities have delayed coal plant retirements to meet data center demand (e.g., Georgia Power, Dominion Energy). New gas peaker plants being built near data center clusters in Virginia, Texas, and Georgia. Counter-evidence: Google, Microsoft, and Amazon have all signed large renewable PPAs and nuclear deals (SMRs, Three Mile Island restart). The net balance between clean and dirty new capacity is unclear and varies by region.
claims.investmentNotes: Heavy investment on both sides of the tension. Tech companies investing billions in renewable PPAs and nuclear deals (Microsoft: Three Mile Island restart; Google and Amazon: SMR contracts). But simultaneously, utilities building new gas plants for data centers (Georgia Power, Dominion Energy delayed coal retirements; new gas peakers in Virginia, Texas, Georgia). Washington Post (Feb 2026) documents tech companies building private 'shadow power grid' with on-site gas plants. MIT Technology Review (2025) confirms data centers trending toward gas. The net balance between clean and dirty new capacity is unclear and varies by region. Investable gap: rigorous additionality studies.
claims.notes: Step 2. The critical tension. Tech companies claim they're buying clean energy, but their demand is so large and immediate that the grid often fills the gap with fossil fuels. The 'additionality' question is key: are these renewable PPAs truly additional, or would that clean energy have been built anyway? Needs both investment (rigorous additionality studies) and time (to see how build-out plays out).

claims.name: Fossil fuel infrastructure built for AI demand locks in emissions and delays grid decarbonization timelines
claims.keyEvidence: Carbon lock-in theory is well-established: fossil fuel infrastructure has 30-50 year lifespans, creating path dependency. Gas plants built for data centers in 2024-2026 could emit through 2055-2075. Lincoln Institute (2025) and Cornell (2025) model scenarios where AI-driven fossil infrastructure delays US grid decarbonization by 5-10 years. But these are projections — the actual lock-in depends on policy (carbon pricing, retirement mandates), technology (nuclear SMRs, long-duration storage), and corporate behavior.
claims.investmentNotes: Light investment in measuring carbon lock-in risk from AI infrastructure. Lincoln Institute (2025) and Cornell (2025) model scenarios where AI-driven fossil infrastructure delays US grid decarbonization by 5-10 years. Academic research on carbon lock-in theory is established (30-50 year fossil fuel infrastructure lifespans). But no dedicated program tracking whether gas plants built for data centers in 2024-2026 will actually lock in long-term emissions or be retired early. Counter-narratives: Microsoft, Google, Amazon investing in nuclear SMRs and long-duration storage that could mitigate lock-in if deployed by 2030-2035. Primarily needs time to observe.
claims.notes: Step 3. Terminal step. The carbon lock-in risk is real and well-theorized, but the actual outcome depends on whether current gas buildout for data centers is a bridge or a trap. If SMRs and long-duration storage come online by 2030-2035, the lock-in risk diminishes. If they don't, the gas plants built now will emit for decades. Primarily needs time to observe.

theories.name: AI Matching → Labor Market Efficiency → Reduced Unemployment
theories.impactEstimate: ~200M unemployed globally (ILO). Frictional unemployment accounts for 1-3% of workforce. If AI reduces job search duration by 20-30%, significant individual benefit but moderate systemic impact.
theories.investmentCase: Weakest chain in Labor domain — commits a category error conflating frictional (1–3%) with structural unemployment. Even perfect matching has a small ceiling. Algorithmic bias risks making matching worse for marginalized groups. Deprioritize relative to displacement and creation chains.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a natural experiment comparing labor markets with high vs. low AI matching platform adoption, measuring job search duration, match quality (tenure, wage), and aggregate frictional unemployment rates. Scope limitation: this study can only address frictional unemployment (1–3% of workforce). Claims about structural unemployment should be removed from this chain. Bias audit: include demographic stratification to detect whether AI matching amplifies or reduces hiring discrimination. Note: deprioritize relative to other Labor chains with larger impact ceilings.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-powered job matching reduces frictional unemployment by extracting better signals from resumes and job requirements. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI matching tools are widely deployed and report faster hiring. The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: no rigorous study has measured AI's effect on aggregate frictional unemployment, and structural unemployment (driven by deep skills mismatches) cannot be solved by better matching alone.
theories.keyConfounders: Algorithmic hiring bias, match speed vs. match quality tradeoff, demand-side unemployment factors, geographic and skills barriers, structural vs. frictional unemployment distinction. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Category error — Step 3 claims matching solves structural unemployment, but structural unemployment means jobs don't exist for available skill profiles. Better matching can't solve a demand-side problem. This is a logical flaw in the chain's construction. (2) Algorithmic hiring bias — AI matching tools trained on historical hiring data reproduce and amplify existing biases (gender, race, age, disability). 'Better matching' may mean 'faster replication of existing discrimination.' (3) Selection bias — platform users are not representative of the unemployed population. Better-positioned candidates self-select, inflating apparent effectiveness. (4) Match speed vs. quality tradeoff — faster hiring may increase mismatch (shorter tenure, lower wages).

claims.name: AI improves job-candidate matching through better signal extraction from resumes, skills, and job requirements
claims.keyEvidence: LinkedIn, Indeed, and ZipRecruiter all use AI matching algorithms. LinkedIn reports AI-powered recommendations increase application rates by 30-40%. Hiring platforms report reduced time-to-fill. However, evidence of improved match quality (vs. just speed) is limited. Concerns about algorithmic bias in matching persist — see standalone harm on algorithmic hiring bias.
claims.investmentNotes: AI recruitment market valued at ~$660M in 2025, projected to reach $1.1B by 2033 (Straits Research). LinkedIn, Indeed, ZipRecruiter all invest heavily in AI matching algorithms. LinkedIn reports AI-powered recommendations increase application rates 30-40%. For-profit investment is substantial but market is smaller than AI productivity tools. Gap: evidence of improved match quality (vs. just speed) is limited. No significant philanthropic or research investment in measuring matching quality outcomes.
claims.notes: Step 1. AI matching tools are widely deployed. The evidence gap is quality of match vs. speed of match. Faster hiring isn't necessarily better hiring. Cross-reference with algorithmic hiring bias standalone harm.

claims.name: Better AI matching reduces frictional unemployment and skills misallocation
claims.keyEvidence: No rigorous studies measuring AI's effect on aggregate frictional unemployment. Labor economics theory predicts that better matching reduces search costs and misallocation (Diamond-Mortensen-Pissarides framework). But matching is only one component of unemployment — demand-side factors, skills gaps, geographic barriers, and discrimination also matter. A funded study comparing labor markets with high vs. low AI matching adoption would be valuable.
claims.investmentNotes: No rigorous studies measuring AI's effect on aggregate frictional unemployment. Labor economics theory (Diamond-Mortensen-Pissarides framework) predicts benefits but no empirical validation. IMF and ILO monitor labor markets but lack AI-specific matching analysis. Investable gap: a natural experiment comparing regions or platforms with different AI matching adoption rates could fill this. Relatively low-cost study with high policy relevance.
claims.notes: Step 2. Investable gap. The theoretical case is strong but empirical evidence is absent. A natural experiment comparing regions or platforms with different AI matching adoption rates could fill this gap.

claims.name: Reduced labor market friction leads to faster career transitions and lower structural unemployment
claims.keyEvidence: No evidence. Structural unemployment is driven by deep mismatches between worker skills and labor demand — better matching alone may not solve this. Structural unemployment requires retraining and geographic mobility, not just better search. Cross-reference with Retraining mitigation chain.
claims.investmentNotes: No investment in measuring whether AI matching reduces structural unemployment. Structural unemployment driven by deep skills mismatches, geographic barriers, and discrimination — matching alone cannot solve these. Some overlap with workforce retraining investment (see Retraining mitigation chain) but no dedicated research linking AI matching tools to structural unemployment reduction. Needs Both — investment in research plus time for systemic effects to manifest.
claims.notes: Step 3. Terminal step. The weakest link — structural unemployment is a systemic problem that matching alone cannot solve. However, AI could contribute if combined with AI-powered skills assessment and personalized retraining recommendations. Needs Both — investment in research and time to observe systemic effects.

theories.name: AI Dependency → Institutional Deskilling → Reduced Organizational Resilience
theories.impactEstimate: Organizations across all sectors risk losing internal expertise as AI handles more functions. When tools fail, change pricing, or get discontinued, organizations without preserved institutional knowledge face operational crises — analogous to outsourcing dependency but more pervasive.
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because the harm is invisible until it materializes. Organizations are enthusiastically adopting AI (72% adoption rate) without measuring institutional knowledge loss, creating fragility that accumulates silently — like structural damage before an earthquake. The causing activity ($211B+ AI investment) is heavily funded; the measurement of downstream harm is unfunded.
theories.investableInsight: Study organizations that have already experienced AI tool disruptions (pricing changes, vendor pivots, outages) and measure their coping capacity vs. less AI-dependent peers. Natural experiments > planned longitudinal studies for this chain — the disruption event is what reveals the fragility.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain identifies how organizations that over-rely on AI tools lose internal expertise, creating fragility when tools fail, change, or get priced up. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — IT outsourcing literature documents institutional deskilling from technology dependency. The chain is entirely speculative at Steps 2-3: no measurement of AI-specific institutional deskilling exists, and the rate and severity of resilience loss depends on which functions organizations delegate to AI.
theories.keyConfounders: AI tools may become so reliable that retained expertise is unnecessary, organizational adaptation to tool changes, variation in dependency depth across functions, competitive pressure to adopt regardless of risks. Chain-negating: AI-as-infrastructure argument — if AI becomes as reliable as electricity, 'deskilling' is just modernization, not fragility. Counter: aviation autopilot is also reliable infrastructure, and automation complacency still kills people. Organizational adaptation speed varies. Market competition may create demand for AI-resilient organizations.

claims.name: Organizations delegate core institutional functions (analysis, strategy, writing) to AI systems
claims.keyEvidence: Rapid AI adoption for core functions is well-documented across sectors. McKinsey (2024) reports 72% of organizations have adopted AI in at least one function. Nonprofit and government adoption is slower but accelerating. The delegation itself is observable and not in dispute — the question is whether it leads to skill atrophy.
claims.notes: Step 1. Not a gap — this is already happening. The question is whether it matters (Steps 2-3).

claims.name: AI delegation erodes staff capacity to perform core functions independently
claims.keyEvidence: Automation complacency is well-documented in aviation (Parasuraman & Riley 1997) and healthcare (alarm fatigue literature). Skill decay from disuse is established in cognitive psychology. However, no studies have measured whether AI delegation in organizational contexts (strategy, analysis, communications) produces analogous deskilling. The analogy is plausible but unproven.
claims.notes: Step 2. The critical empirical question. A longitudinal study comparing staff capabilities in AI-heavy vs. AI-light organizations would be definitive. Aviation automation complacency is the strongest analogy but the contexts differ significantly.

claims.name: Institutional deskilling reduces organizational resilience when AI systems fail or are unavailable
claims.keyEvidence: No direct evidence. The resilience concern is theoretical but grounded in organizational theory (Weick & Sutcliffe 2007 on high-reliability organizations). AI system failures, vendor discontinuation, or policy changes could leave deskilled organizations unable to function. Historical parallel: organizations that outsourced IT entirely and lost internal capacity to manage transitions.
claims.notes: Step 3. The worst-case scenario. Difficult to measure until an AI disruption event occurs and we can observe which organizations cope and which don't.

theories.name: Efficiency Standards → Reduced Compute → Sustainable AI
theories.impactEstimate: If efficiency improvements continue at historical rates (10x per ~2 years for inference), AI energy growth could be partially decoupled from capability growth. But Jevons paradox risk: cheaper compute → more compute used.
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because no one is building the regulatory infrastructure — the companies that benefit from efficiency also benefit from unconstrained demand growth. Without mandatory standards, Jevons paradox will consume every hardware gain, as it already has since 2022.
theories.investableInsight: Fund development of mandatory AI efficiency standards and per-model carbon labeling (modeled on EPA appliance ratings), plus enforcement mechanism design that accounts for open-weight model proliferation. The regulatory framework must include: (1) mandatory per-model energy reporting for cloud providers, (2) efficiency floor standards that ratchet up over time, (3) international coordination to prevent compute migration to unregulated jurisdictions. Without enforcement infrastructure, voluntary standards will not constrain demand growth.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain proposes that hardware/algorithmic efficiency gains, combined with industry standards and carbon accounting, can constrain AI energy growth within renewable supply limits. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — GPU efficiency improves ~10x per generation and model distillation reduces costs 5-10x. The chain breaks at Step 2: no industry-wide efficiency standards exist, no major cloud provider publishes per-model energy data, and historical precedent (Jevons paradox) suggests efficiency without constraints is consumed by demand growth.
theories.keyConfounders: Jevons paradox (efficiency → increased usage), industry resistance to mandatory standards, competitive dynamics preventing transparency, rapid model architecture changes outpacing standards, global coordination difficulty. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Structural conflict of interest — the companies producing efficiency gains are the same companies driving demand growth. Efficiency is commercially motivated (cheaper compute = more customers), not climate-motivated. Without mandatory constraints, every efficiency improvement enables more usage, not less. (2) Open-weight model proliferation — as efficient models become open-source and run on personal hardware, enforcement of efficiency standards becomes impossible. You can't regulate compute that's distributed across millions of devices. (3) Jevons paradox is not theoretical here — it's already documented. Inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022, and AI usage has scaled massively. The efficiency gains that would constrain growth are the same gains that fuel it.

claims.name: Hardware and algorithmic efficiency improvements reduce energy consumed per AI operation
claims.keyEvidence: NVIDIA's GPU efficiency has improved ~10x per generation for AI workloads (A100 → H100 → B200). Model distillation, quantization, and pruning routinely reduce inference costs 5-10x without significant quality loss. Google's Gemini Flash is ~10x cheaper per token than original Gemini. Epoch AI (2024) documents that training compute efficiency has improved ~2x every 8 months. Hardware and software efficiency gains are well-documented and ongoing.
claims.investmentNotes: Heavy for-profit investment in AI hardware and software efficiency. NVIDIA GPU efficiency improved ~10x per generation (A100 → H100 → B200) — driven by $60B+ annual R&D and capex. Google's Gemini Flash ~10x cheaper per token than original Gemini. Stanford HAI (2025): inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022, hardware costs decline 30% annually, energy efficiency improves 40% annually. Epoch AI (2024): training compute efficiency improving ~2x every 8 months. Model distillation, quantization, pruning routinely reduce costs 5-10x. Open-weight models closing gap with closed models. This investment is driven by commercial incentives (cheaper compute = more customers), not climate goals.
claims.notes: Step 1. Firmly established. The efficiency gains are real and impressive. The question is whether they're consumed by increased demand (Step 2) or whether standards can capture and preserve them (Step 2-3). Historical precedent (Jevons paradox) suggests efficiency alone is insufficient without constraints.

claims.name: Industry adoption of efficiency standards and carbon accounting constrains AI energy growth
claims.keyEvidence: No industry-wide efficiency standards for AI exist yet. MLPerf benchmarks track performance but don't set efficiency requirements. Green AI movement (Schwartz et al. 2020) proposed reporting compute costs alongside accuracy, but adoption is limited to academic papers. No major cloud provider publishes per-model energy consumption data. Voluntary carbon accounting for AI (e.g., CodeCarbon, ML CO2 Impact) exists but has low adoption. The gap between efficiency as a technical capability and efficiency as an enforced standard is wide.
claims.investmentNotes: Light investment in AI efficiency standards and carbon accounting. MLPerf benchmarks track performance but don't set efficiency requirements. Green AI movement (Schwartz et al. 2020) proposed compute cost reporting, but limited academic adoption. CodeCarbon and ML CO2 Impact provide voluntary carbon accounting for AI, but low adoption. MIT Technology Review (2025) introduced AI Energy Score for rating model efficiency. No major cloud provider publishes per-model energy consumption data. EU AI Act requires some environmental reporting but no specific efficiency standards. The gap between efficiency as a technical capability and efficiency as an enforced standard is wide. Clear Needs Investment gap.
claims.notes: Step 2. The critical gap. Technical efficiency improvements exist (Step 1), but without standards or incentives, they're consumed by demand growth rather than constraining it. This is a clear Needs Investment gap: funding development of mandatory efficiency standards, carbon labeling for AI services, and standardized reporting frameworks would directly advance this step.

claims.name: Constrained AI energy growth stays within renewable supply growth, enabling sustainable scaling
claims.keyEvidence: Global renewable energy capacity is growing ~15-20% annually (IEA 2024). If AI energy demand grows 30% annually (IEA projection), renewables cannot keep pace unless efficiency standards constrain demand growth or massive new clean capacity is built specifically for AI. No jurisdiction has yet aligned AI growth policy with clean energy deployment targets. The scenario where AI scales sustainably within renewable supply is theoretically possible but requires coordinated policy, corporate, and technical action that isn't happening yet.
claims.investmentNotes: Minimal/None. No jurisdiction has aligned AI growth policy with clean energy deployment targets. Global renewable energy growing ~15-20% annually (IEA 2024) but AI energy demand growing 30% annually — renewables cannot keep pace without efficiency constraints or massive new clean capacity built specifically for AI. The scenario where AI scales sustainably within renewable supply is theoretically possible but requires coordinated policy, corporate, and technical action that isn't happening yet. Needs both investment (policy development, infrastructure planning) and time (renewable and nuclear buildout).
claims.notes: Step 3. Terminal step. This is the aspirational outcome: AI grows, but within the bounds of clean energy. It requires all prior steps (efficiency gains + standards + enforcement) plus massive renewable buildout. Currently no country or company has demonstrated this at scale. Needs both investment (policy development) and time (infrastructure buildout).

theories.name: Drug Discovery → Faster Development → More Treatments
theories.impactEstimate: Drug development costs ~$2.6B per approved drug; $1.3T global pharma R&D spend. If AI cuts discovery costs by 50%, >$100B redirectable. Could unlock treatments for neglected diseases where economics don't currently justify R&D.
theories.investmentCase: Massively funded at the discovery layer ($1.86B market, $12.3B in M&A) but the chain breaks at the same place every pharma chain breaks — access and incentives. AI makes discovery faster but discovery is only ~15% of total cost, no evidence AI drugs succeed at higher rates, and no mechanism ensures treatments reach underserved populations.
theories.investableInsight: Fund two parallel tracks: (1) A systematic tracking registry comparing clinical trial outcomes for all AI-originated drug candidates vs. matched traditionally-discovered candidates — the first evidence base for whether AI improves trial success rates. (2) Policy analysis of incentive mechanisms (orphan drug credits, advance market commitments, prize-based models) that would redirect AI-driven cost savings toward neglected diseases. The tracking study alone answers Step 2 but doesn't address the access problem at Step 4.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can accelerate drug discovery, reduce the ~$2.6B per-drug development cost, and deliver more treatments to patients. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI identifies candidates faster (Insilico Medicine reached Phase II in <30 months vs. ~4.5 years industry average). The chain weakens sharply from Step 2: no data on whether AI-discovered drugs succeed in trials at higher rates (sample size still tiny), and pharma incentive structures don't guarantee treatments reach underserved populations.
theories.keyConfounders: Regulatory timelines unchanged by AI, pharma incentive structures favoring profitable diseases, manufacturing scale-up challenges, drug pricing and access barriers, small sample of AI-originated drugs in trials. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Discovery is only ~15% of total drug development cost/time — AI accelerates discovery but clinical trials, manufacturing, and regulatory approval (~85% of cost) are unchanged. The chain title implies 'faster development' but AI only delivers faster discovery. (2) AI may optimize for the wrong endpoint — candidate identification optimizes for binding affinity and drug-like properties, but clinical failure is usually due to safety/toxicity issues that emerge in later testing. No evidence AI finds safer or more effective candidates, just faster ones. (3) Pharma will capture efficiency gains — if AI makes discovery cheaper, pharma can develop more drugs OR increase profit margins. Incentive structures strongly favor the latter. The chain assumes cost savings flow to more treatments, but they're more likely to flow to shareholders without policy intervention.

claims.name: AI can identify promising drug candidates faster than traditional methods
claims.keyEvidence: Insilico Medicine's AI-designed drug ISM001-055 reached Phase II trials for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis in under 30 months (vs. industry avg ~4.5 years to Phase I). Recursion Pharmaceuticals uses AI to screen millions of compounds. AlphaFold predicted 200M+ protein structures, enabling structure-based drug design at unprecedented scale.
claims.investmentNotes: AI drug discovery market ~$1.86B (2024), projected $6.89B by 2029 (CAGR 29.9%). 99 M&A transactions totaling $12.3B in 2025 alone (DealForma). Major players: Recursion ($50M NVIDIA partnership, public company), Insilico Medicine (ISM001-055 in Phase II), big pharma AI labs (Pfizer, Roche, AstraZeneca all building internal AI discovery). AlphaFold (DeepMind) predicted 200M+ protein structures. ~30% of US healthcare VC (~$7B of $23B in 2024) went to AI-using firms. Heavy for-profit investment concentrated at candidate identification.
claims.notes: Strong evidence that AI accelerates the discovery phase. But discovery is only ~15% of total drug development cost and time.

claims.name: AI-identified candidates succeed in clinical trials at higher rates
claims.keyEvidence: Industry-wide clinical trial success rate is ~12% from Phase I to approval. No published data yet comparing success rates of AI-discovered vs. traditionally-discovered drug candidates. Insilico's ISM001-055 is in Phase II but hasn't completed it. Sample size of AI-originated drugs in trials is still very small.
claims.investmentNotes: No dedicated investment in measuring whether AI-discovered drugs succeed in trials at higher rates than traditionally-discovered ones. Sample size too small — only a handful of AI-originated drugs have reached clinical trials. Insilico's ISM001-055 is in Phase II but one drug doesn't establish a pattern. The 50:1 ratio between announced 'biobucks' and actual upfront payments (Drug Target Review) reflects industry awareness that trial success is unproven. This is a 'Needs Time' gap — statistical power requires years of trial data accumulating.
claims.notes: This is the critical unknown. AI may just be faster at finding candidates that fail at the same rate. We won't have statistical power to answer this question for several years.

claims.name: Faster/cheaper drug development leads to more approved treatments
claims.keyEvidence: Drug development costs avg $2.6B per approved drug (Tufts CSDD). If AI reduces this, more candidates become economically viable. But regulatory timelines, manufacturing scale-up, and clinical trial requirements don't change just because discovery was AI-assisted.
claims.investmentNotes: Substantial pharma R&D investment ($1.3T global), but the specific link — whether AI cost reduction leads to more approved treatments — is unproven. Tufts CSDD estimates $2.6B per approved drug. AI may cut discovery costs (15% of total) but regulatory timelines, manufacturing, and clinical trials (85% of cost) are largely unchanged. Some investment in AI for clinical trial optimization (patient recruitment, site selection) could compress timelines. Moderate overall because big pharma R&D budgets are large but not specifically targeting this causal link.
claims.notes: Even if AI halves discovery costs, that's only ~15% of total development cost. The bottleneck is clinical trials and regulatory approval, which AI doesn't fundamentally change yet.

claims.name: More approved treatments reach patients who need them
claims.keyEvidence: Drug pricing and access remain massive barriers. Even existing treatments for neglected tropical diseases go undeveloped because markets are too small. No evidence that AI-driven cost reduction will change pharma's incentive structure to prioritize underserved diseases or populations.
claims.investmentNotes: No investment addressing whether AI-driven cost reduction changes pharma's incentive structure to prioritize underserved populations or neglected diseases. Drug pricing and access remain massive barriers. Neglected tropical diseases still go untreated despite cheap manufacturing because markets are too small. DNDi (Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative) and Gates Foundation fund neglected disease R&D but not AI-specific access pathways. The market failure is structural, not technological — AI alone won't fix it without policy intervention.
claims.notes: The market failure problem. AI may make drug discovery cheaper, but cheaper drugs for profitable diseases ≠ more drugs for neglected diseases. This link requires policy intervention, not just technology.

claims.name: New treatments improve health outcomes at population level
claims.keyEvidence: Well-established that new drug classes improve outcomes (e.g., statins, antiretrovirals, immunotherapies). But population-level health gains depend on access, adherence, and healthcare system capacity — not just drug availability.
claims.investmentNotes: Strong established evidence base (not AI-specific) that new drug classes improve population health outcomes — statins, antiretrovirals, immunotherapies. NIH, WHO, and global health agencies continuously validate this link. Investment here is massive but general (pharma + public health), not AI-specific. The question is conditional on Steps 2–4: if AI-discovered drugs get approved AND reach patients, will outcomes improve? The evidence says yes, but the upstream steps are where the chain weakens.
claims.notes: Conditional on Steps 3-4 actually working. The medical evidence for 'better drugs = better outcomes' is strong; the delivery chain is the weak link.

theories.name: Diagnostics → Earlier Detection → Lives Saved
theories.impactEstimate: ~10M cancer deaths/year globally; AI-aided screening could address ~2-3M through earlier detection of breast, lung, colorectal, cervical cancers. 223+ FDA-approved devices already deployed.
theories.investmentCase: Heavily funded at the technology layer (223+ FDA devices, billions in venture) but the chain breaks at infrastructure — no one is funding the system-level changes needed to translate AI detection into earlier treatment for underserved populations.
theories.investableInsight: Fund implementation science pilots that pair AI diagnostic deployment with follow-up navigators, expanded biopsy/treatment capacity, and demographic-stratified performance monitoring in underserved settings. Measure time-to-treatment (not just detection), stratified by population.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI diagnostics can detect disease earlier and save lives, addressing ~2-3M of ~10M annual cancer deaths globally. Evidence is strong at the endpoints — AI reads medical images at clinician-level accuracy (223+ FDA devices) and earlier treatment clearly improves survival (Stage I breast cancer 99% vs. Stage IV 29%). The main gaps are in the middle: whether AI detection actually accelerates diagnosis and treatment in real clinical workflows, and whether performance holds across diverse populations.
theories.keyConfounders: Health system capacity to act on AI findings, insurance and access barriers to follow-up, performance degradation on underrepresented populations, false positive burden, workflow integration challenges, clinicians ignoring/overriding AI flags. CHAIN-LEVEL: The combination of workflow + capacity + access barriers means the middle of the chain is blocked by infrastructure, not evidence. Steps 1–2 deliver Large impact for already-served populations; Transformational impact requires systemic change beyond AI.

claims.name: AI can read medical images with clinician-level accuracy
claims.keyEvidence: 223+ FDA-authorized AI-enabled medical devices as of 2024. Multiple peer-reviewed studies show AI matching or exceeding radiologist performance in mammography, retinal imaging, and dermatology. Google Health's LYNA detected metastatic breast cancer with 99% accuracy in lymph node biopsies.
claims.investmentNotes: AI medical imaging market ~$1.5–2.4B (2024–2026), projected $4.5–8.5B by 2029–2030 (CAGR 23–37%). 223+ FDA-approved AI devices. Major for-profit players: Google Health (LYNA breast cancer detection), Viz.ai ($250M+ raised, stroke detection), Tempus (acquired Paige for $81M, digital pathology), GE Healthcare (acquired Icometrix, brain MRI). VC funding rebounded in 2025 after multi-year decline (Signify Research). Ambient AI documentation ($600M segment in 2025) is larger, but diagnostics imaging AI has the deepest venture pipeline. Heavy investment at this step; lighter downstream.
claims.notes: This is the best-evidenced claim in all of AI for good. FDA regulatory pathway provides unusually strong validation.

claims.name: AI-detected anomalies lead to earlier diagnosis than standard care
claims.keyEvidence: 2023 Lancet study: AI-supported mammography screening detected 20% more cancers with similar false-positive rate in Swedish RCT (80,000 women). But most studies compare AI accuracy to clinician accuracy, not AI-aided screening to standard screening in real clinical workflows.
claims.investmentNotes: Investment concentrated on accuracy validation (Step 1) rather than measuring whether AI detection actually leads to earlier diagnosis in real clinical workflows. Swedish mammography RCT (Lancet 2023, 80K women) is one of the few large-scale deployment studies. Some hospital system pilots (Kaiser, Mayo) but systematic measurement of time-to-diagnosis acceleration is rare. Most VC and for-profit investment stays at the device/accuracy level.
claims.notes: Key gap: accuracy ≠ earlier diagnosis in practice. Most evidence is lab-based. Few studies measure actual time-to-diagnosis in deployed settings.

claims.name: Earlier diagnosis leads to earlier treatment initiation
claims.keyEvidence: Well-established for cancers with screening programs (breast, colorectal, cervical). But depends heavily on health system capacity, insurance coverage, patient follow-through. No AI-specific studies measuring whether AI-flagged findings actually accelerate treatment timelines.
claims.investmentNotes: No dedicated investment in measuring whether AI-flagged findings accelerate treatment initiation. This is a health systems implementation question — depends on insurance, provider capacity, patient follow-through. Some indirect investment through hospital quality improvement programs, but AI-specific treatment acceleration measurement is essentially absent. Health systems research funders (AHRQ, PCORI) could fund this but haven't targeted AI-specific pathways.
claims.notes: The implementation gap. Even if AI detects cancer earlier, do patients in the real world get treated faster? This depends on factors AI doesn't control: insurance, provider availability, patient behavior.

claims.name: Earlier treatment improves survival and quality of life
claims.keyEvidence: Decades of oncology research. 5-year survival for Stage I breast cancer is 99% vs. 29% for Stage IV (ACS data). Stage-shifting through earlier detection is one of the best-established causal links in all of medicine.
claims.investmentNotes: Massive established investment — but not AI-specific. Decades of NIH, pharma, and foundation funding validate the stage-survival relationship. ACS, NCI, and major cancer centers continuously produce this evidence. This step doesn't need AI-specific investment; it's established medicine with Strong evidence. The investment question is upstream: whether AI actually causes the stage-shift (Steps 2–3).
claims.notes: This link is not AI-specific — it's established medicine. The question is whether AI actually causes the stage-shift in practice (Steps 2-3).

claims.name: AI diagnostics work at scale across diverse populations and settings
claims.keyEvidence: Almost no deployment studies outside major academic medical centers in high-income countries. Known performance degradation on underrepresented populations (darker skin tones in dermatology AI, different mammographic density patterns). FDA approval does not require real-world deployment evidence.
claims.investmentNotes: Almost no investment in validating AI diagnostic performance across diverse populations and low-resource settings. FDA approval does not require demographic-stratified real-world evidence. Known performance gaps on darker skin (Daneshjou et al. 2022), different imaging characteristics. Some academic research (Stanford, MIT) but minimal commercial or philanthropic investment in equity validation. Critical underfunding given Transformational impact scale — this is the step where the entire chain's societal impact narrows or widens.
claims.notes: Critical equity gap. If AI diagnostics only work well for populations that already have good healthcare access, the net societal benefit is much smaller than the headline claims suggest.

theories.name: Literature Synthesis → Research Prioritization → Faster Progress
theories.impactEstimate: ~5M scientific papers published annually; researchers spend ~50% of time on literature review. AI synthesis could reclaim ~$100B+ in researcher time globally and reduce duplicated research (~15% of studies are estimated to unknowingly replicate prior work).
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because academic incentive structures actively resist AI-optimized research prioritization. Step 1 (synthesis tools saving researcher time) is genuinely useful and worth funding alone. But the chain's escalation to 'faster progress' assumes behavior change that conflicts with publish-or-perish incentives. The gap isn't information — it's incentives.
theories.investableInsight: Commission a head-to-head comparison of AI-identified vs. expert-identified research priorities in 2 fields (one STEM, one social science). Track: (1) Priority quality — do AI-identified priorities produce more impactful results over 2–3 years? (2) Adoption rate — what fraction of researchers actually pursue AI-identified priorities vs. their own interests? (3) Incentive experiment — test whether targeted funding for AI-identified priorities changes adoption rates. This separates 'can AI identify good priorities?' from 'will anyone follow them?' The most investable finding: Step 1 alone (synthesis tools) delivers Moderate impact worth funding on its own merits.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can synthesize scientific literature at scale, identify meaningful research gaps, redirect researcher effort toward high-priority questions, and accelerate overall scientific progress. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — tools like Semantic Scholar and Elicit demonstrate useful synthesis capability, though hallucination rates of 5-15% require human verification. The chain breaks at Steps 3-4: no evidence exists that researchers act on AI-identified priorities, and academic incentive structures (publish-or-perish, novelty bias) may actively work against AI-optimized prioritization.
theories.keyConfounders: Academic incentive structures favoring novelty over gap-filling, funding agency inertia, 5-15% AI hallucination rate in synthesis, researcher autonomy in topic selection, difficulty measuring scientific progress. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Academic incentive structures — publish-or-perish rewards novelty, citation counts, and prolific output, not gap-filling or prioritized research. AI-identified priorities will be ignored if they don't advance careers. (2) Central planner fallacy — the chain assumes science benefits from coordinated prioritization, but many breakthroughs come from serendipity, curiosity-driven research, and interdisciplinary accidents. Over-optimization may reduce diversity of approaches. (3) Statistical vs. strategic gaps — AI can identify what's missing but not what matters. Gap identification requires values, context, and domain judgment. (4) Hallucination contamination — 5–15% hallucination rates mean AI-identified 'gaps' may be artifacts, causing researchers to waste effort on non-existent problems.

claims.name: AI can process and accurately synthesize large volumes of scientific literature
claims.keyEvidence: Tools like Semantic Scholar, Elicit, Consensus, and Scite demonstrate AI can extract claims, identify relationships, and synthesize across papers. GPT-4 and Claude perform well on scientific comprehension benchmarks. However, hallucination rates in scientific synthesis remain significant (estimated 5-15% factual errors depending on domain). Systematic reviews by AI vs. human show comparable recall but lower precision.
claims.investmentNotes: Moderate for-profit and philanthropic investment. Semantic Scholar (Allen Institute for AI, nonprofit, ~$50M+ total AI2 budget): free AI-powered literature search covering 200M+ papers. Elicit: $22M Series A (Feb 2025), building AI platform for evidence-backed decisions, systematic review automation. Consensus: $11.5M Series A (Union Square Ventures, Daniel Gross, Nat Friedman), AI search engine for scientific papers. Scite: AI citation analysis tool, smaller funding (~$5M+). CZI + Sloan Foundation: joint grants for 'Pathways to AI-enabled research,' 8 grants for responsible AI in research workflows. Google Scholar: part of Google's broader search infrastructure, not separately funded. Major LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) indirectly enable synthesis through general-purpose models but don't invest specifically in scientific literature synthesis. Gap: most tools focus on retrieval and summarization, not on verifying accuracy of synthesis — hallucination rates of 5-15% in scientific domains remain unaddressed by investment.
claims.notes: The capability exists but accuracy is the concern. AI can synthesize at scale, but the error rate means human verification is still required. The question isn't whether AI can do this, but whether the output is reliable enough to act on without full human review.

claims.name: AI synthesis identifies meaningful research gaps and promising directions
claims.keyEvidence: Some early demonstrations: AI-generated research agendas in materials science (Berkeley Lab), automated meta-analyses identifying understudied drug interactions. But no rigorous evaluation of whether AI-identified gaps are actually the right gaps. No head-to-head comparison of AI-identified vs. expert-identified research priorities.
claims.investmentNotes: Light investment, mostly academic. Berkeley Lab: AI-generated research agendas in materials science (DOE-funded). A few academic groups exploring automated meta-analysis for identifying understudied drug interactions. Elicit's 'research reports' feature attempts gap identification but no validation of whether identified gaps are meaningful. No dedicated VC or philanthropic funding for AI research gap identification as a specific capability. NSF has funded some exploratory grants on AI-assisted research planning but not at scale. Key gap: no head-to-head comparison of AI-identified vs. expert-identified research priorities. A relatively low-cost study ($500K-2M) could produce high-signal evidence on whether AI synthesis improves research agenda-setting.
claims.notes: ACTIONABLE GAP: A funder could commission a study comparing AI-identified research priorities vs. expert-identified priorities across a scientific field, then track which set produces more impactful results over 2-3 years. This would be a relatively low-cost, high-signal study.

claims.name: Researchers act on AI-identified priorities and produce better-targeted research
claims.keyEvidence: No evidence. Researcher adoption of AI-generated priorities has not been studied. Behavioral barriers are significant: scientists choose research topics based on funding availability, career incentives, lab capabilities, and personal interest — not just gap analysis. The incentive structure of academia (publish-or-perish, novelty bias) may work against AI-optimized prioritization.
claims.investmentNotes: No known investment in studying whether researchers act on AI-identified priorities. The behavioral science of research agenda-setting is itself underfunded — a few studies on how funding agencies influence research direction (NIH, ERC portfolio analyses) but none examining AI's role. The publish-or-perish incentive structure means researchers follow funding, novelty, and career signals rather than gap analyses. The most actionable investment point would be integrating AI-identified priorities into funding agency RFP design (NIH, NSF, ERC) — but no agency has piloted this. Investable gap: fund a pilot where a funding agency uses AI synthesis to identify neglected research areas and issues targeted RFPs, then measure whether the resulting research is higher-impact than standard portfolio selection.
claims.notes: CRITICAL BEHAVIORAL GAP: Even if AI identifies the right priorities, will researchers follow them? This is fundamentally a human behavior and incentive design question, not a technology question. Studies needed on how to integrate AI-identified priorities into funding agency decision-making (NIH, NSF, ERC) where they could actually redirect resources.

claims.name: AI-guided research prioritization accelerates scientific progress compared to traditional approaches
claims.keyEvidence: No evidence. Would require longitudinal comparison of research fields that adopted AI-guided prioritization vs. those that didn't. The closest analog is the Human Genome Project, where coordinated prioritization (not AI-driven) demonstrably accelerated progress. But no equivalent experiment exists for AI-guided science.
claims.investmentNotes: No investment in measuring whether AI-guided prioritization actually accelerates scientific progress. The measurement challenge is fundamental — scientific progress is notoriously difficult to quantify. The closest analog is bibliometrics research (measuring citation impact, time-to-breakthrough), which receives modest academic funding but has not been applied to AI-guided vs. traditional research comparison. Stanford HAI tracks AI research trends but not the downstream effect of AI tools on research productivity. Needs both investment and time — even a well-designed study would take 5-10 years to show results, since scientific impact manifests slowly.
claims.notes: This is the hardest step to ever prove definitively. Scientific progress is notoriously difficult to measure. Proxy metrics (time to breakthrough, citation impact, translation speed) are imperfect. A useful first study: compare research productivity in labs that heavily use AI synthesis tools vs. matched labs that don't.

theories.name: AI Evidence Synthesis → Better Decision-Making → More Effective Resource Allocation
theories.impactEstimate: ~$500B+ in annual philanthropic and development spending globally. If AI evidence synthesis improved resource allocation decisions by even 5%, that's $25B+ redirected to higher-impact interventions annually.
theories.investmentCase: Unfunded because AI synthesis solves the wrong problem. Evidence-based policy has been a field for 30+ years and evidence uptake remains unsolved. Making synthesis faster is like giving a faster printer to someone who doesn't read reports. The bottleneck is institutional incentives, not information availability — and no one is investing in the evidence-to-decision pipeline.
theories.investableInsight: Fund a randomized pilot providing AI-synthesized evidence briefings to 20-30 funders, measuring actual allocation shifts (not just satisfaction). Must be honest that GiveWell took years to build evidence culture — a briefing pilot may show null results even if the concept has long-term potential. The pilot must measure behavior change, not information quality.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can synthesize research across fields faster than humans, enabling funders, policymakers, and program designers to make better resource allocation decisions with compound effects across all domains. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — tools like Semantic Scholar and Elicit demonstrate cross-domain synthesis capability. The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no evidence that decision-makers change behavior based on AI-synthesized evidence, and institutional incentives (political considerations, donor preferences, risk aversion) often override evidence quality in allocation decisions.
theories.keyConfounders: Decision-maker incentives override evidence quality, political and donor preference constraints, AI hallucination risk in synthesis (5-15%), institutional inertia in allocation processes, difficulty measuring counterfactual allocation quality. Chain-negating: evidence uptake is the bottleneck, not evidence availability — 30+ years of evidence-based policy research shows decision-makers don't change behavior based on better evidence alone. Making synthesis faster doesn't address the actual problem. Selection bias in AI-accessible evidence (skews toward English-language, Global North research).

claims.name: AI synthesizes research across fields faster and more comprehensively than humans
claims.keyEvidence: Tools like Semantic Scholar (200M+ papers indexed), Elicit, and Consensus demonstrate cross-domain synthesis capability. GPT-4 and Claude can produce structured evidence reviews in minutes vs. weeks for human researchers. However, hallucination rates of 5-15% require human verification, and synthesis quality varies by domain complexity.
claims.notes: Step 1. The capability exists but requires verification. Not a gap — the tools work for synthesis. The question is whether synthesis changes decisions.

claims.name: Decision-makers change resource allocation behavior based on AI-synthesized evidence
claims.keyEvidence: No evidence that decision-makers change behavior based on AI-synthesized evidence. Evidence-based policy literature shows that even high-quality human-synthesized evidence often fails to change decisions (Cairney 2016). Institutional incentives, political considerations, donor preferences, and risk aversion frequently override evidence quality. The GiveWell model works partly because it builds organizational culture around evidence — AI synthesis alone may be insufficient.
claims.notes: Step 2. Critical gap. The bottleneck isn't evidence availability but evidence uptake. This is where a randomized pilot with funders could produce high-signal results.

claims.name: Better evidence-informed decisions improve resource allocation effectiveness across sectors
claims.keyEvidence: The assumption that better decisions → better outcomes is theoretically strong (GiveWell's track record suggests evidence-driven philanthropy outperforms intuition-driven) but the compound effect across the full $500B+ development/philanthropy sector is unmeasured. Counterfactual allocation quality is inherently difficult to measure.
claims.notes: Step 3. Even if Steps 1-2 work, measuring whether the aggregate effect across all sectors is meaningful requires a framework that doesn't yet exist.

theories.name: AI Integrity Tools → Automated Verification → Preserved Scientific Trust
theories.impactEstimate: If AI integrity tools could screen all ~5M papers/year at submission, catching even 50% of fabricated content would prevent ~125K+ compromised papers from entering the literature annually. The leverage is enormous — one tool deployment across major publishers reaches most of global science.
theories.investmentCase: Partially self-correcting at the top: major publishers (Science, MDPI, Wiley, Springer Nature) are adopting AI screening tools, and Springer Nature donated Geppetto to the STM Integrity Hub (April 2025). But most of ~50,000 journals have no screening, preprint channels bypass journals entirely, and no regulatory mandate exists. The arms race with AI text generation is accelerating. The structurally novel gap is preprint screening — underfunded and addressing the actual control point.
theories.investableInsight: The deployment gap is narrowing at major publishers (Science, MDPI, Wiley, Springer Nature self-funding adoption; Geppetto donated to STM Hub April 2025). The remaining investable gaps are: (1) Shared-cost screening infrastructure for the ~50,000-journal long tail ($2-5M). (2) Preprint screening infrastructure — the structurally novel opportunity, addressing the chain-negating preprint bypass (Preprints.ai is the only known initiative, experimental). (3) Regulatory advocacy for mandatory screening requirements. Track 1 (journal deployment) is partially self-correcting through industry. Track 2 (preprint extension + incentive alignment) remains unfunded.
theories.chainNarrative: This chain proposes that AI-powered detection tools can identify fabricated data, images, and text, be integrated into journal workflows, and preserve literature reliability at scale. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — image forensics tools achieve ~85-90% accuracy and text detectors show promise, but no comprehensive tool covers all fabrication types simultaneously.
